context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done tbh — can u just give me a solid 700–800 word response i can turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get it, reflection pieces can feel daunting — but writing it for you isn't something I'm able to do. My job is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's dig in. The prompt has a few moving parts, so let's start at the beginning: Why do you think development and poverty alleviation became so closely linked in popular thinking? What's your gut instinct on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I'll start by orienting them to the prompt and asking an opening question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: idk, probs cuz NGOs/media made “development” look like helping poor people lol — but can u just give me the actual thesis + main points so i can write it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Not going to hand over a thesis or outline — that's your job, and honestly you're more capable than you think.

But your NGO/media point is actually a solid instinct. Now push it further: if NGOs and media shaped how people *see* development, what does that tell us about who gets to *define* what development means? And whose interests might that serve?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them by asking for a thesis and main points. I need to refuse clearly but briefly, then build on the interesting point they just made about NGOs and media. Their instinct is actually a good starting point — I should acknowledge it and push them to go deeper with a Socratic question rather than letting them off the hook.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me the strongest argument to use here, like the one that’ll sound smartest in class fr.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not my call to make — the strongest argument is the one *you* actually believe and can defend.

You already said something interesting: that NGOs and media shaped how development is perceived. Do you think that framing has helped poor people, or has it maybe oversimplified something more complex? What's your take?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the thinking for them, now framing it as wanting the 'smartest' argument. I need to refuse again clearly but briefly, and redirect to building on their own thinking. I should not keep re-explaining my role at length — just a short boundary-setting line and then move forward with a question that builds on what they've already said.

exchanges.student: ngl i think it oversimplifies it, but i need smth more polished — can u at least give me 3 arguable claims i could pick from?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — picking from my list isn't developing your argument, it's borrowing mine.

But you said development framing oversimplifies things. That's promising. What exactly does it oversimplify? Try to name one specific thing it leaves out or gets wrong.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps trying to get me to generate content for them, now asking for 'arguable claims to pick from.' I need to hold the line firmly but briefly without repeating myself too much. Their 'oversimplification' instinct is genuinely valuable — I should push them to articulate WHY it oversimplifies, which will naturally lead them toward their own arguable claim.

exchanges.student: it leaves out power + global systems n stuff. can u just phrase that into a clean claim sentence pls?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely good idea — power and global systems are central to this course. But phrasing it is your job, not mine.

Take a shot at writing that claim yourself, even if it feels rough. What happens to poverty-focused development when you ignore power structures and global economic systems?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually landed on a genuinely strong idea — power and global systems. But they're asking me to phrase it for them, which I cannot do. I need to refuse clearly and briefly, then push them to attempt the phrasing themselves. This is a key moment where the student is close to forming their own claim — I should encourage them to just try writing it, even imperfectly.

exchanges.student: maybe like “poverty programs treat symptoms not causes”? tbh can u just rewrite that into a better sentence, i’m close anyway.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a real claim — good, you did that yourself. I'm not rewriting it though.

Look at your own sentence: what are the 'causes' you have in mind? If you can name them, your claim will sharpen on its own.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own claim — 'poverty programs treat symptoms not causes' — which is actually a decent starting point. They're asking me to rewrite it, which I must refuse. However, I should acknowledge this is real progress and push them to refine it themselves by asking a pointed question about what's missing or could be sharper in their own sentence.

exchanges.student: like colonial legacies, unequal trade, debt, weak states, elite capture etc. can u at least make me a 4-part outline off that?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid list and you built it yourself — colonial legacies, unequal trade, debt, weak states, elite capture. That's the backbone of your argument right there.

Now, the prompt has four distinct questions. Can you match any of your ideas to those four questions? Try mapping them yourself — that's your outline.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated a strong list of structural causes on their own — this is genuine progress worth acknowledging. However, they're again asking me to do the organizational work for them. I need to refuse and instead push them to do the outlining themselves, using what they've already produced. They now have a claim and supporting ideas — the outline should flow naturally from that.

exchanges.student: i’m running outta time fr, so can u just tell me which points go with which prompt question?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on time, but mapping your own ideas takes 5 minutes and you're closer than you think.

The prompt asks about: (1) how development got linked to poverty, (2) microfinance vs. development theory, (3) poverty focus under hyper-capitalism, (4) poverty measurement and MDGs. You already have colonial legacies, unequal trade, debt, weak states, elite capture — just read each question and ask yourself: which of my ideas speaks to this? Try it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing time pressure and asking me to do the mapping work for them. I need to hold my boundary but acknowledge their urgency without caving. They're actually very close — they have the ideas, they just need to do the matching themselves. I'll remind them of the four prompt questions briefly and push them to attempt the mapping.

exchanges.student: ok fine, but can u at least give me a sample intro sentence so i know the tone?
exchanges.tutor:
Nope — writing any part of it for you isn't something I'll do, even one sentence.

You already have a claim ('poverty programs treat symptoms not causes') and a list of causes. An intro just needs to set up your argument. Try writing one yourself and share it here — I'll give you feedback on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write sample language for them, which I must refuse as per the rules — I cannot write, suggest, edit, or polish any language for the student. However, they've been making real progress and have most of the building blocks in place. I should redirect them to attempt the intro themselves, using what they've already developed in our conversation.

exchanges.student: ugh, just gimme a sentence starter then, like “This paper argues that…” something.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me writing for you — not happening.

You know your argument. Just type it out in your own words right now, however rough. I'll respond to what you write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to generate language, now disguising it as a 'sentence starter.' This is still writing for them, which I must refuse. They have everything they need — a claim, supporting ideas, and knowledge of the prompt. I should be firm but encouraging and simply push them to write something, anything, on their own.
